1: "Not without results" (ou kenē gegonen) — kenē = empty/vain; the perfect tense (gegonen) states a completed fact with present standing. Paul will use kenē again (1 Corinthians 15:14) for gospel proclamation that would be empty without the resurrection. Here it asserts the abiding fruitfulness of his visit.
2: "Suffered and been treated outrageously in Philippi" — propathontes and hybristhentes (aorist participles): past completed actions summarizing Acts 16. Hybrizō (to outrage) is a strong term for deliberate public humiliation. "We dared" (eparrēsiasametha) — parrēsia = frank, free, bold speech; the noun names the courage of speaking plainly regardless of risk.
3: The three negatives are formal disclaimers of the sophist's standard vices: not from "error" (planēs — moral delusion), not from "impure motives" (akatharsias), not through "deceit" (dolō — bait, trick). These three categories correspond to the accusations commonly made against wandering teachers in the Greco-Roman world.
4: "Approved by God" (dedokimasmetha hypo tou theou) — perfect passive of dokimazō: tested and having been found genuine, approved. The metallurgical image is of assaying metal — heating to certify purity. "Who tests our hearts" (tō dokimazonti tas kardias hēmōn) — present participle; God's testing is ongoing, making human approval irrelevant.
5: "Flattery" (kolakeias) — NT hapax; the obsequious behavior of those who say what audiences want to hear for personal advantage. "Mask to cover up greed" (prophasis pleonexias) — prophasis = pretext/cover-story; pleonexia = greediness. Together: a cover story concealing desire for financial or social gain.
6: "Assert our authority" (en barei einai hōs Christou apostoloi) — literally "to be in weight/heaviness"; baros = burden/weight, both financially (demanding material support) and socially (pulling rank). Paul deliberately waived the authority-claim that was his right as an apostle.
7: The significant textual variant: nēpioi (infants, the harder reading) vs ēpioi (gentle). If nēpioi is original (lectio difficilior principle), the mixed metaphor is dramatic: infants who became a nursing mother. A nursing mother (trophos) is warm, physically intimate, and self-giving — no itinerant sophist would have used this image for himself.
8: "Share with you... our lives as well" (tas heautōn psychas) — psychas = souls/lives; Paul offered not just a message but his whole person. "Because we loved you so much" (dioti agapētoi hēmin egenēthēte) — perfect passive: you became and remain beloved to us. The community was not a mission target but persons who became dear.
9: "Toil and hardship" (kopos kai mochthos) — a fixed pair (also 2 Corinthians 11:27; 2 Thessalonians 3:8): kopos = wearying labor, mochthos = hardship/drudgery. "Working night and day" (nyktos kai hēmeras) — genitive of time throughout the night and day; probably tent-making (Acts 18:3), which distinguishes Paul from philosophers who received patron support.
10: "Holy, righteous and blameless" (hosiōs kai dikaiōs kai amemptōs) — three adverbs of manner: hosiōs (devoutly toward God), dikaiōs (righteously toward people), amemptōs (blamelessly, without accusation). The triad covers the vertical God-relation and horizontal human-relation of integrity.
11: Three participles for fatherly formation: parakaloumenoi (exhorting/encouraging), paramythoumenoi (comforting/consoling — the warmer word), martyromenoi (earnestly urging, bearing witness-like testimony to). Each describes a distinct mode of apostolic care for individual community members.
12: "Live lives worthy of God" (peripatein axiōs tou theou) — peripateō (walk/conduct life) is Paul's preferred ethical verb; axiōs means proportionately, fittingly — in a manner commensurate with. "His kingdom and glory" — both dimensions shape the ethical demand: present participation in the kingdom-reality and future eschatological glory.
13: "Word of God... at work in you" (ho kai energeitai en hymin tois pisteuousin) — energeitai is present tense: the word is actively energizing (our "energy" is from this root). The word received is not inert information but a dynamic agent working in recipients. "Not as a human word" — Paul distinguishes the divine origin from the human medium.
14: "Suffered from your own people" (hypo tōn idiōn symphyletōn) — symphyletai = fellow-tribesmen, those of the same ethnic/civic group. The Thessalonians' persecutors are their own Gentile neighbors, creating suffering-solidarity with the Judean Jewish-Christian communities who faced opposition from their own people.
15: "Who killed the Lord Jesus and the prophets" — the sequence is striking: Jesus named first, then the prophets; chronologically reversed but theologically ordered. The death of Jesus is the climax of a pattern of prophetic rejection embedded in Israel's history (cf. Matthew 23:37). Paul speaks as a Jew himself using intra-Jewish prophetic critique.
16: "Wrath has come upon them at last" (ephthasen de ep' autous hē orgē eis telos) — ephthasen is aorist: arrived, reached. Eis telos = finally, to the uttermost, completely. The verb phthanō (to arrive, come upon) generates debate: prophetic perfect (future wrath spoken as arrived) or reference to a specific event Paul knew. The wrath is presented as a completed arrival, not merely imminent threat.
17: "Orphaned by being separated from you" (aporphanisthentes aph' hymōn) — aporphanizō = to be made an orphan; NT hapax. The parting-of-parent-from-child image recalls the nursing-mother and father metaphors of 1 Thessalonians 2:7-11; the separation is experienced as bereavement, not inconvenience. "For a short time in person, not in thought" (pros kairon hōras, prosōpō ou kardia) — the physical absence does not sever the relational bond; the idiom "face but not heart" makes visible what is invisible.
18: "Satan blocked our way" (enekopsen hēmas ho Satanas) — enkoptō (to cut into, block, hinder): a military term for cutting a road to stop an advancing army. Satan is presented as an active personal agent who physically impeded apostolic travel; Paul does not speculate about how. The acknowledgment of Satanic agency in thwarting ministry plans is direct and without embarrassment.
19: "Our hope, our joy, or the crown in which we will glory" (elpis hē chairē kai stephanos kauchēseōs) — the three nouns of eschatological celebration: hope, joy, and the victory-crown. Stephanos (wreath/crown) is the athletic or civic crown of victory or honor, not the royal diadem (diadēma). The crown of boasting (kauchēseōs) is the honor that Paul will carry to present before the Lord at the parousia — and that honor is the community itself.
20: "You are our glory and joy" (hymeis gar este hē doxa hēmōn kai hē chara) — the declarative statement completes the rhetorical question of 1 Thessalonians 2:19. The community is not merely the occasion of Paul's joy but constitutes it: este = you are (present: now and ongoing). The apostle's eschatological glory is his converts standing before Christ. The community is the apostle's crown.
